did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an ensemble of gradient boosting algorithms (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost) for six-class skin lesion classification on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset. It extracts handcrafted features (color histograms, Haralick texture, Hu moments) and clinical metadata to perform the classification task.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents HCHS-Net, a novel multimodal framework for skin lesion classification. It details original experimental work including the design of handcrafted feature extraction modules (Color, Haralick, Shape), integration of clinical metadata, and implementation of an ensemble gradient boosting classifier. The study reports original results from experiments on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, including accuracy metrics (97.76%), ablation studies, and comparisons with deep learning baselines, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: HCHS-Net: A Multimodal Handcrafted Feature and Metadata Framework for Interpretable Skin Lesion Classification
brief_summary: This study introduces HCHS-Net, a lightweight and interpretable multimodal framework for six-class skin lesion classification using the PAD-UFES-20 dataset. The model extracts 116-dimensional visual features via Color, Haralick texture, and Shape modules, which are concatenated with 12-dimensional clinical metadata to form a 128-dimensional input vector. Classification is performed using an ensemble of XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost algorithms with majority voting. HCHS-Net achieved 97.76% accuracy with only 0.25 million parameters, outperforming deep learning baselines like VGG-16 and ResNet-50 while requiring significantly fewer parameters. The framework demonstrated perfect recall for melanoma and nevus classes and an inference time of 0.11 ms per image, enabling real-time CPU-based deployment. These results indicate that domain-informed handcrafted features combined with metadata offer a computationally efficient and interpretable alternative to heavy deep learning models for point-of-care diagnostics.

lesion_types_studied.details: Pre-cancerous lesion class in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset; 730 original samples (31.77%); augmented to 2500 samples.
Malignant skin cancer class in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset; 845 original samples (36.77%); augmented to 2500 samples.
Highly malignant lesion class in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset; 52 original samples (2.26%); augmented to 2500 samples; achieved 100% recall.
Benign melanocytic growth (mole) class in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset; 244 original samples (10.62%); augmented to 2500 samples; achieved 100% recall.
Malignant skin cancer class in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset; 192 original samples (8.35%); augmented to 2500 samples.
Benign warty growth class in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset; 235 original samples (10.23%); augmented to 2500 samples.

all_task_types.target: Six-class skin lesion diagnostic categories (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, Nevus, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Seborrheic Keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs six-class classification of skin lesions using the PAD-UFES-20 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories using an ensemble of gradient boosting algorithms (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost) with majority voting on class probability distributions. The task is explicitly defined as 'six-class skin lesion classification' with reported metrics including accuracy (97.76%), recall, specificity, and AUC-ROC.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: A publicly available clinical skin lesion dataset collected at the Federal University of Espírito Santo (UFES), Brazil, comprising smartphone-captured clinical images under uncontrolled conditions with comprehensive patient metadata (demographics, lifestyle, lesion dimensions, symptoms).

use.training: Used for training the HCHS-Net framework after data augmentation to balance classes (augmented to 15,000 total samples, 2,500 per class).
use.validation: Used for validation via ablation studies and hyperparameter tuning (specific split counts not reported).
use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation, reporting accuracy, recall, specificity, and inference time.
use.other: Served as the primary benchmark for comparing against deep learning baselines (VGG-16, ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B2) and other published methods.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To partition the dataset into training (80%) and test (20%) sets while maintaining patient-level independence and class distribution consistency.
To standardize continuous clinical metadata features (Age, Diameter) to zero mean and unit variance.
To measure agreement beyond chance between classifier predictions and ground truth.
To evaluate the quality of binary and multi-class classifications.
To assess the discrimination ability of the classifiers across all diagnostic categories.
To analyze per-class performance, including recall, precision, and specific misclassification patterns (e.g., ACK vs BCC).
To evaluate the individual and combined contributions of feature modules (Color, Haralick, Shape, Metadata) on model performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., t-tests, ANOVA) with p-values. Statistical robustness was validated using 95% Confidence Intervals (CIs) computed via empirical bootstrapping with 1000 iterations, which confirmed the stability of the accuracy but did not constitute a formal significance test comparing groups.

PAD-UFES-20.experiment: Six-class skin lesion classification on test set (20% stratified split)
PAD-UFES-20.notes: HCHS-Net achieved a 2.53% accuracy gain from metadata integration compared to the Visual Only baseline. Melanoma recall had a 95% CI of 92.4–100.0% due to class imbalance (52 original samples). BCC recall had a 95% CI of 94.8–98.1%. HCHS-Net required 60–97x fewer parameters than deep learning baselines and was 28–63x faster in inference.

architecture_used.main_model: HCHS-Net (Multimodal Handcrafted Feature and Metadata Framework with Ensemble Gradient Boosting)
architecture_used.pretraining: Not applicable for the proposed model; Deep learning baselines (VGG-16, ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B2) were initialized with ImageNet pre-trained weights.
architecture_used.input_size: 128-dimensional feature vector (116-dimensional visual features concatenated with 12-dimensional clinical metadata); Input images resized to 224 × 224 × 3 for baselines.

feature_extraction_modules.color_module: Multi-channel histogram analysis (HSV color space) yielding 96-dimensional vector.
feature_extraction_modules.haralick_module: Gray-level co-occurrence matrix (GLCM) texture descriptors yielding 13-dimensional vector.
feature_extraction_modules.shape_module: Hu moment invariants encoding morphological properties yielding 7-dimensional vector.

architecture_details.metadata_integration: 12-dimensional vector comprising patient demographics (Age, Gender) and lesion characteristics (Diameter 1, Diameter 2, Symptoms, Lifestyle), normalized via Z-score.
architecture_details.fusion_method: Majority voting / Averaging of class probability distributions from the three gradient boosting models.

training_details.optimizer: Not applicable for proposed model (gradient boosting); Adam (lr=1e-4, beta1=0.9, beta2=0.999) for baselines.
training_details.loss_function: Not applicable for proposed model; Categorical Cross-Entropy for baselines.
training_details.training_schedule: Not applicable for proposed model; Max 50 epochs with early stopping (patience=4) and learning rate reduction for baselines.

classification_or_output_head.method: Ensemble averaging of class probability distributions from CatBoost, XGBoost, and LightGBM.
classification_or_output_head.final_prediction: Argmax of the averaged probability distribution.

preprocessing_techniques: Gaussian filtering (2D kernel, σ = 1.0, 5 × 5 size) for noise reduction and artifact suppression
Spatial normalization via bilinear interpolation to resize images to 224 × 224 pixels
RGB to HSV color space conversion for color histogram extraction
L2-normalization of color histograms for brightness invariance
Conversion to grayscale for Haralick texture feature extraction
Z-score normalization of continuous clinical metadata features (Age, Diameter)
Data augmentation (Horizontal Flip, Vertical Flip, Rotation ±30°, Brightness Adjustment 0.8-1.2, Affine Transform) applied exclusively to the training set to balance class distribution

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Offline augmentation strategy applied exclusively to the training partition (80% of original data) to balance class distribution. Each of the six diagnostic classes was expanded to contain 2500 samples, yielding a balanced training dataset of 15,000 images. The test set (20%) remained unaugmented.

conclusions: HCHS-Net, a lightweight multimodal framework using handcrafted features and clinical metadata, achieved 97.76% accuracy on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, outperforming deep learning baselines (VGG-16, ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B2) while requiring significantly fewer parameters (0.25 M vs. 8.16–24.14 M).
The integration of clinical metadata provided a 2.53% accuracy gain over visual features alone, confirming the complementary value of multimodal inputs in skin lesion classification.

HCHS-Net demonstrated superior computational efficiency with an inference time of 0.11 ms per image, enabling real-time classification on standard CPUs without GPU acceleration, which is 28–63 times faster than the tested deep learning models.

The model achieved perfect recall (100%) for Melanoma and Nevus classes and high specificity (99.55%), indicating reliable discrimination at safety-critical diagnostic boundaries, although the Melanoma recall confidence interval was broad (92.4–100.0%) due to class imbalance.

Ablation studies confirmed that the Color module contributed the most to performance among visual features, with the full combination of Color, Haralick, and Shape features yielding 95.23% accuracy before metadata integration.

Misclassifications predominantly occurred between visually similar classes (e.g., Actinic Keratosis and Basal Cell Carcinoma), while the model successfully maintained perfect discrimination between pre-cancerous lesions and melanoma.

The framework offers superior interpretability compared to black-box deep learning models, as its handcrafted features directly correspond to established dermatological criteria (e.g., ABCD rule), facilitating clinical adoption and regulatory compliance.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding generalization to a single benchmark dataset and identifies future work priorities including multi-dataset validation, hybrid feature architectures, and integration with teledermatology platforms.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single dataset (PAD-UFES-20) collected in Brazil using smartphone cameras under uncontrolled conditions; generalization to high-resolution dermoscopic archives (e.g., ISIC) or diverse global populations cannot be assumed without external validation.

The dataset exhibits severe class imbalance (Melanoma: 2.26%, BCC: 36.77%); while data augmentation balanced the training set, the small original sample size for Melanoma (52 samples) results in a wide confidence interval for its recall (92.4–100.0%).
The model relies on global feature descriptors computed over entire images without explicit lesion segmentation, which may incorporate irrelevant background information into the feature vector.
The ensemble classification strategy uses equal weighting across classifiers (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost); adaptive weighting mechanisms were not explored and could potentially optimize performance.
Data augmentation was applied exclusively to the training set to balance classes; the authors note this strategy may not fully capture the complete spectrum of real-world clinical variability.
The paper does not report formal hypothesis testing (p-values); statistical robustness was validated using 95% Confidence Intervals computed via empirical bootstrapping with 1000 iterations.

Misclassifications predominantly occur between visually similar classes (e.g., Actinic Keratosis and Basal Cell Carcinoma), reflecting genuine diagnostic overlap in dermatological practice rather than model failure.

The framework achieves high computational efficiency (0.11 ms inference time, 0.25M parameters) enabling CPU-based deployment, but this comes at the cost of relying on handcrafted features that may lack the representational power of deep learning for complex patterns.
